summary: Shaw describes his seemingly supernatural ability to trigger software bugs in every system he touches, from Pearson's royalty payment system to Google Kubernetes to his bank's internal databases. He recounts a pattern spanning decades where routine actions like changing an address or adding bold text cause mysterious failures that support teams can't explain. He argues this curse actually made him a better programmer, since he learned to write defensively by assuming everything would break. He also criticizes companies for expecting users to file detailed bug reports and for getting hostile when bugs are pointed out.
key_points: Shaw triggers obscure edge-case bugs with routine actions across virtually every platform he uses, from financial systems to developer tools
Companies react poorly to bug reports—Paypal/eBay employees harassed him for pointing out a double-login vulnerability that could enable phishing
He argues bug reporters shouldn't have to navigate complex tracking systems; the company should fill out the ticket and ask the reporter to verify it
His constant exposure to broken software made him a better developer who writes defensively by default
Even developer-oriented tools like Kubernetes failed him repeatedly despite following official documentation exactly
money_quotes: You want to find out if your software works well? Have me try to use it.
I'm just cursed with a weird devil hand that drives software insane is all.
I went from saying Paypal is broken to saying they're fucking terrible programmers.

Oh, and I don't fucking work for you. I'm not going to waste 15-30 minutes navigating your bullshit bug tracker, filling out every random Jira/Bugzilla/Confluence/AgileXPHardon12000 tracking data point just so you can ignore me.
You fill out the damn ticket and ask me if it's right. That's what I do. I don't make other people fill out bug reports. That's just rude.
I'm like the coder from Salusa Secondus who had no idea things weren't supposed to be this broken and ended up just making things solid to avoid it.
I've brought systems to their knees just casually clicking around.
key_insight: Constantly encountering software bugs as a user breeds defensive programming habits, but the tech industry's hostility toward bug reporters discourages the very feedback it needs most.
